title: Daily Health Briefing
intro: Good morning! Today's digest leans on fresh 2026 trials and meta-analyses across your tracked topics — including new rapamycin/senolytic data, a 2026 HIIT meta-analysis, two 2026 pelvic-floor RCTs, the published NEJM rezatapopt results, and the latest on sleep. Sources are linked as chips.

sections.body:
The longevity-drug story got more sober in 2026. The new **RAPA-EX-01** randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (40 sedentary adults aged 65–85, 6 mg once-weekly sirolimus vs placebo alongside a 13-week home exercise program) found rapamycin **did not enhance — and may have slightly blunted — training gains**, while adding more adverse events. The authors' takeaway: regular physical activity remains the first-line strategy for preserving function.

A 2026 review in *Aging-US* reached a similar conclusion: despite strong animal data, there is **no clear clinical evidence** that low-dose rapamycin extends healthspan or slows aging in healthy adults — larger, better-designed human trials are needed (two big NIH/ARPA-H–funded multi-year RCTs are now underway).

**Senolytics** (e.g. dasatinib + quercetin, fisetin) remain proof-of-concept: as of 2026 most trials are still Phase 1/2 with modest human effect sizes, and **no senolytic is FDA-approved** for any aging indication.

- Treat single-drug "longevity" claims with skepticism — the human evidence isn't there yet.
- Exercise still beats every pharmacologic shortcut studied to date.

sources.title: Exercise and Weekly Sirolimus (Rapamycin) in Older Adults: RAPA-EX-01 Randomised, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial
Rapamycin Shows Limited Evidence for Longevity Benefits in Healthy Adults
Bench to bedside: is rapamycin headed for the docTOR?

sections.body:
A new **2026 systematic review and meta-analysis** (19 RCTs) in *Frontiers in Physiology* gives the clearest picture yet for middle-aged and older women:

- HIIT produced a **large, high-certainty improvement in cardiorespiratory fitness** (VO2max; SMD = 1.20, 95% CI 0.86–1.54), consistent across age, body-mass status, and program length.
- But HIIT **alone did not significantly improve muscle strength, flexibility, or sit-to-stand**, with only borderline gains in walking ability.
- Conclusion: HIIT is excellent for the heart and lungs, but **insufficient on its own** for overall physical function.

A 2026 network meta-analysis in women with **PCOS** echoed this — HIIT ranked best for VO2max, fasting insulin, and LDL cholesterol, while moderate-intensity training edged it out for insulin resistance (HOMA-IR).

Practical takeaway: keep the intervals for cardio and metabolic health, but **pair them with resistance (and balance) training** to protect strength and function.

sources.title: Effects of HIIT on cardiopulmonary fitness and physical function in middle-aged and elderly women: a systematic review & meta-analysis
Comparative cardiometabolic effects of high, moderate, and low intensity exercise in PCOS: a network meta-analysis of RCTs

sections.body:
Two **2026 randomized trials** reinforce pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) as a high-value, low-risk first-line tool.

- **JAMA Network Open (Apr 2026):** the *PEFLOW* multicenter RCT (764 first-time mothers across 9 hospitals) found a structured prenatal pelvic-floor program **cut stress urinary incontinence at 6 weeks postpartum to 8.7% vs 13.9%** in controls, and more than doubled the share of women with strong pelvic-floor contractions.
- **Int. Urogynecology Journal (2026):** in women with stress incontinence awaiting surgery, a 6-week **intensive preoperative PFMT** program significantly improved muscle strength and symptom severity (large effect sizes, d ≈ 0.85–0.90) — and **43% postponed their planned surgery**.

The latest Cochrane evidence adds nuance: **direct, isolated, voluntary contractions** beat indirect approaches, and **supervised group vs individual delivery makes little difference**. Biofeedback/e-stim are optional add-ons, not requirements.

Key point: *correct technique + consistency* drives results — and starting in pregnancy can prevent problems later.

sources.title: Pelvic Floor Workout (PEFLOW) for Preventing Stress Urinary Incontinence in Primiparous Women: A Randomized Clinical Trial
Effectiveness of Intensive Preoperative Pelvic Floor Muscle Training in Women with Stress Urinary Incontinence Awaiting Surgery: An RCT
Comparisons of approaches to pelvic floor muscle training for urinary incontinence in women (Cochrane Review)

sections.body:
For **TP53 / p53-mutant** cancers, the big update is that **rezatapopt (PC14586)** — the first-in-class oral p53 reactivator selective for the **TP53 Y220C** mutation — now has **published Phase 1 results in the *New England Journal of Medicine* (Feb 2026)**. In a heavily pretreated population it produced objective responses across tumor types: ORR ~43% in ovarian, **18% in breast**, 60% in endometrial, and ~22% in lung cancer. The pivotal **PYNNACLE Phase 2** basket trial (incl. dedicated breast cohorts spanning HER2+, HER2−, and triple-negative) is enrolling, with primary completion estimated Aug 2026.

For **HER2-positive breast cancer with co-existing TP53 mutations** (a tougher, faster-growing subtype), new preclinical work shows these co-mutant tumors have increased histone acetylation that **sensitizes them to trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd)**, and that **neratinib + T-DXd** is highly effective in mouse models — a combination now in an early-phase NCI trial (NCT05372614).

Important framing: "alternative" here means **emerging, evidence-based, trial-based** options to discuss with an oncologist — not replacements for standard care. p53 remains historically "undruggable," so these mutation-specific advances are genuinely notable.

sources.title: Rezatapopt, a p53 Reactivator, in TP53 Y220C-Mutated Tumors (NEJM 2026;394:872-883)
PYNNACLE: rezatapopt (PC14586) monotherapy in TP53 Y220C-mutated solid tumors (NCT04585750)
HER2;p53 Co-mutated Cancers Show Increased Histone Acetylation and Are Sensitive to Neratinib plus Trastuzumab Deruxtecan

sections.body:
The 2026 consensus still puts **behavior ahead of supplements** for restless or disjointed sleep.

- **CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia)** remains the guideline-recommended first-line treatment (AASM 2021, ACP). It improves sleep efficiency ~8–16% with large, durable effects — roughly **5–7× more effective than supplements**, and gains persist long after treatment ends.
- **Magnesium bisglycinate:** the best-designed modern RCT (Schuster 2025) showed only a **small effect (d ≈ 0.2)** on insomnia severity — ~3.9-point ISI drop vs ~2.3 for placebo, i.e. a real but **not clinically meaningful** ~1.6-point net benefit. Reasonable to try; not a cure.
- **Melatonin:** AASM advises **against routine use for chronic insomnia**; it's best for circadian problems (jet lag, shift work) at low doses (0.5–3 mg). Note: a JAMA analysis found OTC melatonin content ranged −83% to +478% vs label — buy USP/NSF-verified.
- For chronic insomnia needing medication, the **DORA class** (daridorexant, lemborexant, suvorexant) and low-dose doxepin rank above any supplement in the evidence hierarchy.

Best-practice habits:

1. Fixed **wake time**, 7 days/week.
2. Bed for **sleep only**; keep the room cool, dark, quiet.
3. Dim lights and cut screens during a wind-down window.
4. Persistent problems? See a provider to rule out sleep apnea and consider CBT-I (digital CBT-I works when in-person access is limited).

sources.title: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Chronic Insomnia: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
Magnesium Bisglycinate Supplementation in Adults Reporting Poor Sleep: a randomized, placebo-controlled trial (Schuster 2025)
AASM: Missing the mark with melatonin — finding the best treatment for insomnia
